title: Cat Hiding Under Furniture: Causes and When to Worry
meta_desc: Is your kitten hiding under the bed? Learn why cats hide, if it's an emergency, and how to help your feline friend feel safe at home.
content_html:
TL;DR: While hiding is often a normal response to stress or new environments, it can also signal physical illness or pain. Seek immediate veterinary care if your cat stops eating for 24 hours, has difficulty breathing, or shows visible signs of injury.

Why is my cat or kitten hiding?

Hiding is a natural survival instinct for cats and kittens. By retreating to dark, enclosed spaces under furniture, cats feel protected from perceived threats. This behavior is most common in new environments, during loud events like thunderstorms, or when a cat feels physically unwell.

When is cat hiding considered a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low (typically a behavioral response to stress).
  • Contact a veterinarian immediately if your cat is refusing food for more than 24 hours.
  • Seek emergency care if your cat is struggling to breathe or showing signs of injury.

How does sharing a photo or video help a veterinarian triage my cat?

  • Sharing a photo or video provides vital clues about your cat's facial expressions, ear position, and posture.
  • Visual assessments help professionals determine if your cat is exhibiting signs of fear, anxiety, or physical pain.
  • This information helps differentiate between a shy kitten and a pet requiring medical attention.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Fear in cats, stemming from genetic factors, inadequate early socialization, or negative experiences, can manifest as avoidance, withdrawal, and hiding. Stimuli may include unfamiliar people, cats, dogs, noises, places, car rides, veterinary visits, or unfamiliar environments; some cats may be fearful of familiar individuals as well. Initial treatment involves identifying and avoiding fear-inducing situations. Confinement in a safe space with essential resources is crucial to prevent further aggravation. Once calm, gradual reintroduction with counterconditioning using favored items may be attempted. Pharmacological interventions such as fluoxetine, paroxetine, clomipramine, buspirone, or short-term alprazolam may be considered, though individual variability necessitates careful drug and dose selection.

Chapter: Neurology, Dermatology, Psychiatry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: How long should I let my new kitten hide?
faqs.answer: It is normal for a kitten to hide for 24 to 72 hours when first introduced to a new home. Provide them with food, water, and a litter box nearby.

faqs.question: Should I force my cat out from under the couch?
faqs.answer: Avoid reaching in and pulling your cat out, as this can increase their fear and lead to defensive biting or scratching. Instead, use high-value treats or toys to encourage them to come out on their own.

faqs.question: Can hiding be a sign of illness?
faqs.answer: Yes. If a social cat suddenly begins hiding and stops eating or using the litter box, it is often a sign of underlying pain or sickness and warrants a vet visit.
